topic: Tidal M2 period — trivial division of lunar day, dome geometry produces wrong tidal pattern
our_argument_summary: WIN-045 (and the grouped WIN-046/049/050/051) argues that tidal constituent periods are trivial — they follow arithmetically from orbital periods (M2 = half a lunar day, S2 = half a solar day, etc.) and any model that inputs those orbital periods gets them for free. The deeper argument is that the dome's local moon at 2,534 km altitude produces a sharp tidal force spike under the sub-lunar point (dropping to 9% at 5,000 km, 0.6% at equator) rather than the observed two-bulge semidiurnal pattern. The verdict is Self-Contradicted because the dome's own geometry forbids the observed tidal pattern.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitor.py file was found in the current raw-text extraction. The tidal constituent WINs were added in V51.0 (April 2026). Based on the pattern established by previous curmudgeon reviews — where V51.0 additions have no monitoring infrastructure — there is no validation pipeline for tidal periods. No domain in monitor.py handles tidal constituents based on available evidence.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring exists. The tidal constituent WINs appear to have been added to the WIN count as static claims with no automated validation of any kind.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None identified. The dome model does not appear to have published any tidal force derivation from dome geometry — only the periods (which are inputs, not outputs).
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review text does not make specific claims about the dome's code for tidal WINs. This is appropriate since there appears to be no tidal validation code.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py file was located in the raw-text extraction. The tidal constituent WINs (045-051) were added in V51.0. Previous curmudgeon reviews of V51.0 WINs consistently find no monitoring domains for newly added WINs. No evidence of any tidal validation domain in the dome's automation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Tidal constituent periods have been known since at least Doodson (1921) and the harmonic analysis tradition going back to Lord Kelvin in the 1870s. M2 = 12.42h is a fundamental of physical oceanography. The dome model adds no new numerical prediction — it adopts the known periods and relabels the generating mechanism as 'aetheric' rather than gravitational.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: M2 = 12.42 hours has been tabulated since at least Doodson (1921), over a century before the dome model. The dome model's V51.0 (April 2026) simply declared these known values as confirmed WINs.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation connects dome geometry (disc radius 20,015 km, moon altitude 2,534 km, firmament function H(r)) to any tidal constituent period. The periods derive from lunar and solar orbital periods — astronomical parameters that are inputs to the dome model, not outputs of its geometry.

holes_found.description: Cross-reference error: All five tidal WINs (045, 046, 049, 050, 051) reference 'Section 4.5.4' but the tidal pattern self-contradiction section is actually Section 4.5.2 ('Tidal Pattern: One Spike vs. Two Bulges'). Section 4.5.4 is 'Solar Diameter: 50% Variation Through the Day' — completely unrelated. A reader following the cross-reference will be directed to the wrong section. The href (#part4b) correctly targets the self-test tab, but the displayed section number is wrong.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'Section 4.5.4' to 'Section 4.5.2' in the detail_evidence field of WIN-045, WIN-046, WIN-049, WIN-050, and WIN-051 in wins.json.

holes_found.description: The '6.6% variation' figure for globe tidal force is the first-order Taylor approximation (4d/R = 4×6371/384400 = 6.63%). The exact calculation using (F_near - F_far)/F_avg gives ~9.9%. While the argument is unaffected (both are tiny compared to the dome's 99.4% drop at the equator), a dome defender could claim we're understating the globe's tidal force variation to make the contrast look smaller. Using the exact figure would be more rigorous and actually strengthens our argument slightly (even with 10% variation the globe still produces two symmetric bulges).
holes_found.recommendation: Consider changing '~6.6%' to '~10%' with a note that this is the exact near-far ratio, or keep ~6.6% but note it's the first-order approximation. The argument works either way — 10% is still vastly more uniform than the dome's 99.4% drop.

holes_found.description: The claim that Doodson (1921) derives 'all 62 constituents' is imprecise. Doodson's 1921 paper actually derived 389 harmonic constituents in the tide-generating potential. The '62' may refer to a common subset used in practical tidal analysis, but the attribution to Doodson 1921 for exactly 62 is not standard. This appears in WIN-045 and WIN-050.
holes_found.recommendation: Either change to 'derives hundreds of constituents' or 'derives the complete tidal potential including all major constituents.' The exact count is not critical to the argument.

holes_found.description: WIN-045's detail_claim says 'Five new WINs confirm that tidal constituent periods (M2, S2, K1, O1, N2) match dome predictions' — this is the dome's claim, which is fine. But it's only stated in WIN-045 (the group leader), while WIN-046/049/050/051 have individual detail_claims. This inconsistency means WIN-045 carries the argumentative weight for the whole group while the others are somewhat redundant. Not a factual error, but structurally this means WIN-045 must be read first for the others to make sense.
holes_found.recommendation: No change needed — this is the intended group structure with WIN-045 as lead. Just noting for completeness.

stronger_arguments.description: The review could explicitly note that on the dome, tides would vary with LATITUDE (distance from the pole/sub-lunar path), not just time. A city at the disc edge (Antarctic rim) would experience negligible tides (0.2% of peak), while a city near the dome center would experience massive tides. This is testable: tidal amplitudes at Ushuaia, Christchurch, and Cape Town should be dramatically smaller than at Reykjavik if the dome model is correct. In reality, tidal amplitudes at southern hemisphere stations are comparable to northern ones.
sources: NOAA tidal station data for global tide gauge network

stronger_arguments.description: The review could note that the dome model has no mechanism for the 'equilibrium tide' concept — the theoretical tide shape that would exist on a water-covered Earth. On a globe, this is well-defined (two symmetric bulges). On a flat disc with a local moon, the equilibrium shape would be a cone of water piled under the moon with no antipodal bulge. This is a more precise statement of the 'one spike vs two bulges' argument.
sources: Hendershott, M.C. (1981). Long waves and ocean tides. In Evolution of Physical Oceanography, MIT Press.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies the five principal tidal constituents (M2, S2, K1, O1, N2) and their periods. These are indeed the dominant frequencies in global tide data, and recognizing them demonstrates awareness of harmonic tidal analysis. The model also correctly notes that these periods relate to lunar and solar orbital periods — which is exactly what Doodson showed in 1921.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Identifying known tidal constituent periods is taxonomy, not prediction. The periods are derived from astronomical observations (lunar day = 24.84h, solar day = 24h, lunar nodal cycle, etc.) — they are inputs to ANY tidal model, not discriminating outputs. The dome model has never derived a tidal AMPLITUDE, PHASE, or SPATIAL PATTERN from its geometry. The critical test is whether the dome's local moon at 2,534 km can produce the observed two-bulge semidiurnal pattern, and it cannot: the geometry produces one sharp spike, not two equal bulges. Matching the period while getting the pattern catastrophically wrong is like predicting a song's tempo while playing the wrong melody.

deeper_analysis:
The Self-Contradicted verdict is strong and well-supported. The dome's own geometric parameters (moon at 2,534 km, disc radius 20,015 km) produce a tidal force profile that drops to <1% at the equator — this is a geometric fact that no parameter adjustment can fix without moving the moon to globe-like distances. The argument is mass-independent, relying only on the inverse-square law and the dome's stated distances.

The main vulnerability is philosophical: a dome defender could argue that tides are not caused by gravity at all, but by some other 'aetheric' mechanism that the dome hasn't fully described yet. This is an unfalsifiability escape — if the mechanism isn't specified, it can't be tested. Our review implicitly assumes gravitational tides, which is reasonable since the dome model itself uses gravitational language (talking about the moon's 'influence' on tides). But we should be prepared for the retort that 'aetheric tides work differently.'

The counter to this escape is: if the dome claims tidal periods match its predictions, it must have a mechanism that produces those periods. If the mechanism is gravitational, our geometric argument applies. If the mechanism is non-gravitational, the dome must specify it and show it produces both the correct periods AND the correct two-bulge pattern. 'It works differently but we haven't worked out how' is not a prediction.

The cross-reference error (Section 4.5.4 → should be 4.5.2) affects all five tidal WINs and should be fixed. This is a clear moderate-severity issue that could undermine reader trust if noticed.

citations_verified: Doodson, 1921 — DOI 10.1098/rspa.1921.0088 is the correct DOI for 'The Harmonic Development of the Tide-Generating Potential', Proc. Royal Society A, 100, 305-329. Paper does derive tidal constituents from astronomical parameters.
citations_unchecked: The href '#part4b' and 'showTab(selftest)' internal navigation — confirmed to target the self-test tab correctly, but the displayed text 'Section 4.5.4' is wrong (should be 4.5.2)
